inline_review:
commit 69572f5f44083c6369063b003aab7017d13bad9b
Author: Dmitry Ilvokhin <d@ilvokhin.com>

locking: Add contended_release tracepoint to spinning locks

Extend the contended_release tracepoint to queued spinlocks and queued
rwlocks.

> diff --git a/include/asm-generic/qspinlock.h b/include/asm-generic/qspinlock.h
> index bf47cca2c375..8ba463a3b891 100644
> --- a/include/asm-generic/qspinlock.h
> +++ b/include/asm-generic/qspinlock.h
[ ... ]
> @@ -116,6 +117,19 @@ static __always_inline void queued_spin_lock(struct qspinlock *lock)
>  #endif
>  
>  #ifndef queued_spin_unlock
> +
> +DECLARE_TRACEPOINT(contended_release);
> +
> +extern void queued_spin_unlock_traced(struct qspinlock *lock);
> +
> +static __always_inline void __queued_spin_unlock(struct qspinlock *lock)
> +{
> +	/*
> +	 * unlock() needs release semantics:
> +	 */
> +	smp_store_release(&lock->locked, 0);
> +}
> +

Does this cause build failures on architectures that override
queued_spin_unlock?

On architectures like x86_64 with CONFIG_PARAVIRT_SPINLOCKS=y,
queued_spin_unlock is defined as a macro before this header is included. This
causes the preprocessor to skip the declarations of __queued_spin_unlock and
queued_spin_unlock_traced.

Because kernel/locking/qspinlock.c attempts to call __queued_spin_unlock
unconditionally, this would result in an undefined function compilation error.

Conversely, on architectures like PowerPC where queued_spin_unlock is a
static inline function rather than a macro, the ifndef block is not skipped.
This leads to a redefinition error because both the architecture header and
the generic header attempt to define queued_spin_unlock.

>  /**
>   * queued_spin_unlock - release a queued spinlock
>   * @lock : Pointer to queued spinlock structure
> @@ -123,9 +137,16 @@ static __always_inline void queued_spin_lock(struct qspinlock *lock)
>  static __always_inline void queued_spin_unlock(struct qspinlock *lock)
>  {
>  	/*
> -	 * unlock() needs release semantics:
> +	 * Trace and unlock are combined in queued_spin_unlock_traced()
> +	 * so the compiler does not need to preserve the lock pointer
> +	 * across the function call, avoiding callee-saved register
> +	 * save/restore on the hot path.
>  	 */
> -	smp_store_release(&lock->locked, 0);
> +	if (tracepoint_enabled(contended_release)) {

Will this cause linkage failures for loadable modules?

Any loadable kernel module that calls spin_unlock will inline this check and
create a direct reference to the tracepoint's static key. Since the
contended_release tracepoint is never exported via
EXPORT_TRACEPOINT_SYMBOL_GPL(), module builds or loads will fail with
undefined reference errors when spinlocks are inlined.

Additionally, is it safe to inject tracepoint logic into __always_inline
spinlock functions that are called from noinstr contexts?

Kernel entry and NMI handling code utilize raw_spin_unlock in uninstrumentable
(noinstr) sections where RCU is not watching. When spinlocks are inlined,
this causes out-of-line calls to the instrumented queued_spin_unlock_traced()
function from the .noinstr.text section.

Objtool will detect this and fail the build with call leaves .noinstr.text
section warnings. Invoking tracepoints before RCU is watching can also trigger
lockdep splats or crashes.

> +		queued_spin_unlock_traced(lock);
> +		return;
> +	}
> +	__queued_spin_unlock(lock);
>  }
>  #endif
[ ... ]
> diff --git a/kernel/locking/qrwlock.c b/kernel/locking/qrwlock.c
> index d2ef312a8611..5f7a0fc2b27a 100644
> --- a/kernel/locking/qrwlock.c
> +++ b/kernel/locking/qrwlock.c
[ ... ]
> @@ -90,3 +90,19 @@ void __lockfunc queued_write_lock_slowpath(struct qrwlock *lock)
>  	trace_contention_end(lock, 0);
>  }
>  EXPORT_SYMBOL(queued_write_lock_slowpath);
> +
> +void __lockfunc queued_read_unlock_traced(struct qrwlock *lock)
> +{
> +	if (queued_rwlock_is_contended(lock))
> +		trace_contended_release(lock);

Will this spam the trace buffer with spurious events on read-heavy workloads?

The queued_rwlock_is_contended() function checks if the wait_lock is
held by evaluating arch_spin_is_locked(&lock->wait_lock). When a writer is
spinning waiting for active readers to finish, it holds wait_lock for the
entire duration.

If there are multiple active readers, every single reader that drops its read
lock during this window will observe that the lock is contended and emit a
trace_contended_release() event. Only the final reader actually releases the
lock to the waiting writer.

> +	__queued_read_unlock(lock);
> +}
> +EXPORT_SYMBOL(queued_read_unlock_traced);
[ ... ]
output:
{"findings":[{"problem":"Build failure on architectures overriding queued_spin_unlock (e.g., x86_64 paravirt, PowerPC)","severity":"High","severity_explanation":"1. The patch places `__queued_spin_unlock` and `queued_spin_unlock_traced` inside an `#ifndef queued_spin_unlock` block in `include/asm-generic/qspinlock.h`.
2. On x86_64 with `CONFIG_PARAVIRT_SPINLOCKS=y` (the default), `queued_spin_unlock` is defined as a macro prior to inclusion, causing the preprocessor to skip the declaration of `__queued_spin_unlock`. Furthermore, this means the tracepoint logic is completely bypassed on paravirtualized spinlocks.
3. `kernel/locking/qspinlock.c` then attempts to call `__queued_spin_unlock` unconditionally, resulting in an implicit declaration/undefined function compilation error.
4. Conversely, on PowerPC, `queued_spin_unlock` is a `static inline` function rather than a macro, so the `#ifndef` block is not skipped. This causes a conflicting types/redefinition compiler error because both the architecture header and the generic header attempt to define `queued_spin_unlock`."},{"problem":"Loadable module linkage failures due to missing EXPORT_TRACEPOINT_SYMBOL_GPL","severity":"High","severity_explanation":"1. The patch injects `tracepoint_enabled(contended_release)` into the `__always_inline` wrapper functions `queued_spin_unlock`, `queued_read_unlock`, and `queued_write_unlock` in public header files.
2. Any loadable kernel module that calls `spin_unlock` or `read_unlock` will inline this check and create a direct reference to the tracepoint's static key (`__tracepoint_contended_release.key`).
3. The `contended_release` tracepoint is never exported via `EXPORT_TRACEPOINT_SYMBOL_GPL()` (unlike `contention_begin`).
4. Consequently, on kernels configured to inline spinlocks (e.g., `CONFIG_UNINLINE_SPIN_UNLOCK` disabled), building or loading any module that unlocks a spinlock will fail with undefined reference or 'Unknown symbol' errors."},{"problem":"Objtool warnings and RCU-not-watching violations in noinstr contexts","severity":"High","severity_explanation":"1. `queued_spin_unlock` is `__always_inline`, meaning the tracepoint evaluation is forcibly injected into all caller contexts.
2. Kernel entry and NMI handling code utilize `raw_spin_unlock` in uninstrumentable (`noinstr`) sections where RCU is not watching.
3. If spinlocks are inlined (e.g., when `CONFIG_UNINLINE_SPIN_UNLOCK` is disabled, such as on x86_64 with `PREEMPT_NONE=y`), this causes out-of-line calls to the instrumented `queued_spin_unlock_traced()` function from `.noinstr.text`.
4. Objtool will detect this and fail the build with 'call leaves .noinstr.text section' warnings. Furthermore, if tracing is enabled, invoking standard tracepoints before RCU is watching triggers RCU-not-watching lockdep splats or crashes."},{"problem":"Tracepoint spamming and semantic violation in queued_read_unlock_traced","severity":"Medium","severity_explanation":"1. `queued_read_unlock_traced()` fires the tracepoint if `queued_rwlock_is_contended(lock)` returns true.
2. This check evaluates `arch_spin_is_locked(&lock->wait_lock)`. When a writer is spinning waiting for active readers to finish, it holds `wait_lock` for the entire duration.
3. If there are multiple active readers (e.g., 100), every single reader that drops its read lock during this window will observe that the lock is contended and emit a `trace_contended_release()` event.
4. Only the final reader actually releases the lock to the waiting writer. Firing the tracepoint for every reader spams the trace buffer with spurious events and degrades performance on read-heavy workloads."}],"fixes":"","review_inline":"commit 69572f5f44083c6369063b003aab7017d13bad9b
